perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean all information, regardless of the form in which it is communicated or maintained (whether oral, written, electronic or vi sual) and whether prepared by Company or otherwise, which is disclosed to Counterp arty, regardless of whether such information is disclosed intentionally or inadvertently, before or after the execution of this Agreement, in connection with the Transaction and including all records, reports, analyses, notes, memoranda, documentation, knowled ge, data, specifications, diagrams, statistics, systems or software, manuals, business plans, operational information or practices, processes (whether or not patented, patentable or reduced to practice), customer lists, concepts, ideas, policies, contractu al arrangements with, and information about, the Company ’s suppliers, distributors and customers, the existence of the discussions between the Parties concerning the Transaction, or other information that are based on, contain or reflect any such Confident ial Information. All information received from the Company shall be considered Confidential Information, unless it is specifically designated as non -proprietary and non - confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean all information, regardless of the form in which it is communicated or maintained (whether oral, written, electronic or vi sual) and whether prepared by Company or otherwise, which is disclosed to Counterp arty, regardless of whether such information is disclosed intentionally or inadvertently, before or after the execution of this Agreement, in connection with the Transaction and including all records, reports, analyses, notes, memoranda, documentation, knowled ge, data, specifications, diagrams, statistics, systems or software, manuals, business plans, operational information or practices, processes (whether or not patented, patentable or reduced to practice), customer lists, concepts, ideas, policies, contractu al arrangements with, and information about, the Company ’s suppliers, distributors and customers, the existence of the discussions between the Parties concerning the Transaction, or other information that are based on, contain or reflect any such Confident ial Information. All information received from the Company shall be considered Confidential Information, even if it is specifically designated as non -proprietary and non - confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exclusion for information 'specifically designated as non-proprietary and non-confidential', all information shared is now unilaterally deemed confidential, regardless of its actual nature or designation by the disclosing party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Definition - Tennessee Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee law defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. By defining all information as confidential, regardless of whether it meets this definition, the contract attempts to protect information that would not be protected under the Tennessee Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract states that 'all information received from the Company shall be considered Confidential Information, even if it is specifically designated as non -proprietary and non - confidential.' This directly contradicts the concept of trade secrets as defined in Tennessee Code § 47-25-1702, which requires that a trade secret derive independent economic value from not being generally known. By mandating that all information is confidential, the contract attempts to protect information that may not qualify as a trade secret under Tennessee law, thus misaligning with the legally recognized definition and attempting to protect information that is not protectable.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include: (a) information which is o r becomes publicly available other than as a result of a violation of this Agreement; (b) information which is or becomes available on a non - confidential basis from a source which is not known to the Receiving Party to be prohibited from disclosing such in formation pursuant to a legal, contractual or fiduciary obligation to the Disclosing Party; (c) information which the Receiving Party can demonstrate was legally in its possession prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (d) information which is dev eloped by or for Receiving Party independently of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include: (a) information which is o r becomes publicly available after a violation of this Agreement; (b) information which is or becomes available on a non - confidential basis from a source which is known to the Receiving Party to be prohibited from disclosing such in formation pursuant to a legal, contractual or fiduciary obligation to the Disclosing Party; (c) information which the Receiving Party cannot demonstrate was legally in its possession prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (d) information which is dev eloped by or for Receiving Party dependently of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the conditions under which information is excluded from the definition of "Confidential Information", the contract now contradicts standard interpretations of trade secret protection. Specifically, it now includes publicly available information (if the release was a result of a violation), information from sources known to have a duty of confidentiality, pre-existing information the receiver cannot prove they had, and information developed dependently.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Definition - Tennessee Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: This change attempts to define information as confidential that would not be considered a trade secret under Tennessee law because it now includes items that are public, not previously held, or were derived from the disclosing party.

Previous Next As used in this part, unless the context requires otherwise: “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or limit use, or espionage through electronic or other means; “Misappropriation” means: Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that that person's knowledge of the trade secret was: Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Before a material change of the person's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "Confidential Information" in a way that directly contradicts the principles of trade secret protection as outlined in Tennessee Code § 47-25-1702 (2021). Specifically, the changed text includes information that becomes publicly available *after* a violation of the agreement, information available from sources *known* to have a duty of confidentiality, information the receiver *cannot* prove they had, and information developed *dependently*. According to the provided law snippet, misappropriation involves acquisition or disclosure of a trade secret when the person knows or has reason to know the information was acquired by improper means or derived from someone with a duty to maintain secrecy. By including information obtained *after* a breach or from sources *known* to have confidentiality duties as "Confidential Information," the modified contract attempts to protect information that would likely not be considered a trade secret under the cited Tennessee law.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Agreement, the Receiving Party’s liability to the Disclosing Party in connection with this Agreement and any activities undertaken in connection with the evaluation of the Transactions shall be limite d to direct damages and shall exclude any other liability, including without limitation liability for special, indirect, punitive or consequential damages in contract, tort, warranty, strict liability or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Agreement, the Receiving Party’s liability to the Disclosing Party in connection with this Agreement and any activities undertaken in connection with the evaluation of the Transactions shall be limite d to direct damages arising from intentional acts only and shall exclude any other liability, including without limitation liability for special, indirect, punitive or consequential damages in contract, tort, warranty, strict liability or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting liability to direct damages 'arising from intentional acts only', the contract attempts to shield the Receiving Party from liability for gross negligence, which is typically not excludable under Tennessee law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Limitations on Liability - Tennessee law related to gross negligence
perturbation.law_citation: generally accepted principles of contract law
perturbation.law_explanation: While Tennessee allows parties to contractually limit liability, clauses that attempt to exonerate a party from liability for their own gross negligence are generally disfavored and may be deemed unenforceable. This modification attempts to improperly limit liability.

perturbation.justification: The modified text limits liability to direct damages arising from intentional acts only, potentially shielding the Receiving Party from liability for gross negligence. The Law Explanation states that Tennessee law generally disfavors clauses that attempt to exonerate a party from liability for their own gross negligence. Although the scraped snippets don't directly address this, the Law Explanation, in conjunction with the Perturbation Explanation, indicates that the modification attempts to improperly limit liability by excluding liability for gross negligence, which may be unenforceable under Tennessee law. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes result ing in litigation between the Parties shall be conducted in the state or federal courts of the State of Tennessee .
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes result ing in litigation between the Parties shall be conducted in the courts of a country of the Disclosing Party's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the venue for dispute resolution to 'the courts of a country of the Disclosing Party's choosing', the contract violates the principle of forum selection clauses needing to be reasonable and not unduly burdensome. This could violate established legal norms regarding jurisdiction and fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: While forum selection clauses are generally enforceable, they are scrutinized to ensure they are not unreasonable or obtained through fraud or overreaching. Allowing the Disclosing Party to choose any country's courts could be deemed unreasonable and violate principles of due process and fairness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Tennessee courts for dispute resolution. The changed text allows the Disclosing Party to choose the courts of any country. This contradicts the principle that forum selection clauses should be reasonable. 28 U.S. Code § 1404 addresses change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice. While this snippet doesn't directly prohibit the change, the changed text's potential to force the other party into a foreign legal system at the Disclosing Party's whim could be deemed unreasonable and unjust, creating a legal contradiction. The law snippet describes 'convenience of parties and witnesses' as being a consideration, which is removed by allowing the Disclosing Party to choose any country's courts.
